messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.

As an AI assistant, I can help rephrase the following text:

"I am an AI assistant that can provide assistance and support. My purpose is to offer helpful and informative responses to queries and tasks. Feel free to ask me anything and I will do my best to assist you."

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For subjects that already have a consistent schedule, I recommend recording and publishing your content about halfway between each occurrence. This gives your audience enough time to catch the recurring thing your talk about, enough time to send feedback for your episode, and enough time to play your episode before the next recurring thing happens. Because these things could occur on any day of the week, your podcast about it could also be any day of the week that's around the halfway point, but still consider the other factors I've already mentioned.

I suggest that for topics with a regular schedule, it would be beneficial to record and release your content approximately halfway between each instance. This allows your audience sufficient time to follow along with the recurring subject, provide feedback on the episode, and listen to it before the next occurrence. Since these events can happen on any day of the week, it is advisable to choose a podcast release day that falls around the halfway mark, taking into account the other factors mentioned earlier.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You'll see studies that show the most popular days for publishing episodes. But that doesn't mean you should publish on those days or even avoid those days. If you're going to look at when other podcasts publish, look only at other podcasts in your same niche and ignore the rest.

While there are studies that highlight the most popular days for releasing podcast episodes, it's important to remember that this doesn't necessarily mean you should adhere to or avoid those specific days. If you choose to examine the publishing patterns of other podcasts, it would be more helpful to focus solely on those within your niche and disregard the rest.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You already know that your podcast needs top-level cover art to look great in all the podcast apps and directories. You can also further enhance the experience for your audience, create promotional assets, and reinforce your branding by making unique artwork for each episode. But you need to compensate for how each app might display the episode image (especially Apple Podcasts).

It is essential to have high-quality cover art for your podcast, ensuring it appears visually appealing across various podcast apps and directories. Additionally, you can go the extra mile to enhance the overall experience for your audience, create promotional materials, and strengthen your brand by designing unique artwork for each episode. However, it's crucial to consider the differences in how each app displays the episode image, particularly emphasizing the need to optimize it for platforms like Apple Podcasts.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You'll see these (especially the legacy tag from the “iTunes namespace”) at the top level for your whole podcast (called “channel-level”), and they can also be used for individual episodes (called “item-level”). These link out to the cover art hosted somewhere.

These elements, such as the legacy tag from the "iTunes namespace," can be observed at the primary level of your entire podcast, known as the "channel-level." Additionally, they can be utilized for individual episodes as well, which is referred to as the "item-level." These elements serve as links to direct users to the location where the cover art is hosted.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Like with all things Podcasting 2.0, the <podcast:images> tag lets you do more! You can already specify different images for different sizes. For example, an image with only an icon at 150 pixels wide, an image with the title and icon at 600 pixels wide, and an image with more at 1,200 pixels wide. There might be other features in the future, too!

Similar to other aspects of Podcasting 2.0, the <podcast:images> tag offers additional possibilities. Currently, you have the ability to designate various images for different size requirements. For instance, you can have an icon-only image at 150 pixels wide, an image with both the title and icon at 600 pixels wide, and a more elaborate image at 1,200 pixels wide. Furthermore, there might be potential for other features to be introduced in the future as well.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Depending on their device, listeners see your episode artwork in varying sizes. Therefore, make sure your design is effective at both its original size and at thumbnail size. You should include a title, brand, or source name as part of your episode artwork. To avoid technical issues when you update your episode artwork, be sure to:

The size of a listener's device determines how they view your episode artwork, which means it should be visually appealing and functional both in its original size and as a thumbnail. It is important to incorporate a title, brand, or source name into your episode artwork. To prevent technical complications when updating your episode artwork, here are some recommendations to follow:

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Artwork must be a minimum size of 1400 x 1400 pixels and a maximum size of 3000 x 3000 pixels, in JPEG or PNG format, 72 dpi, with appropriate file extensions (.jpg, .png), and in the RGB colorspace. These requirements are different from the standard RSS image tag specifications.

In order to meet the specifications, the artwork for your podcast needs to have a minimum size of 1400 x 1400 pixels and a maximum size of 3000 x 3000 pixels. Additionally, it should be in either JPEG or PNG format, with a resolution of 72 dpi, and saved with the appropriate file extensions (.jpg, .png). Furthermore, it is important to ensure that the artwork is in the RGB colorspace. It's worth noting that these requirements differ from the standard RSS image tag specifications.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For some years now, the only places your episode artwork showed in Apple podcasts were in episode search results, on the Apple Podcasts webpage preview, and when playing an episode from the catalog—not from the library! But apparently, sometime in 2022 (maybe with the release of iOS 16 or one of its updates), Apple ended even these behaviors. We used to wonder which behavior was the “bug”: not displaying episode artwork to followers, or displaying it at all. Now, it seems that displaying it at all was the bug. So now, it seems the only place Apple displays the episode artwork is on the Apple Podcasts episode webpage preview, like this one.

Until now, the episode artwork in Apple Podcasts was only visible in select areas, such as episode search results, the preview page on the Apple Podcasts website, and while playing an episode from the catalog but not from the library. However, it appears that in 2022, possibly with the release of iOS 16 or one of its subsequent updates, Apple has discontinued these display behaviors altogether. In the past, there was confusion about whether the absence of episode artwork for followers was a glitch or if any display at all was a mistake. It now appears that the issue was with displaying the artwork altogether. Consequently, it seems that the only place where Apple currently showcases episode artwork is on the preview page of the Apple Podcasts episode webpage.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: And I'm not referring to the trick of changing your show-level artwork every time you publish a new episode (like No Agenda does)! This might seem to work, but only for the latest episode. It leaves old episodes to show the same image in Apple Podcasts and any app that ignores episode-level images.

I'm not talking about the method of altering your show-level artwork every time a new episode is released (similar to what No Agenda does). While this approach may appear effective, it only applies to the most recent episode. As a result, older episodes would continue to display the same image in Apple Podcasts and any app that disregards episode-level images.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: While Apple Podcasts does not currently support its own <itunes:image> tag for episode artwork, it does support chapters and chapter images! In fact, I used it for this very section of the podcast to demonstrate a different image in a chapter.

Although Apple Podcasts presently does not have its own <itunes:image> tag for episode artwork, it does provide support for chapters and chapter images. Interestingly, I utilized this feature for the current section of the podcast to showcase a distinct image within a chapter.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Chapters are a way of dividing your content in podcast apps. This allows your audience to quickly see your content outline; skip to or away from sections; and see a title, optional image, and an optional hyperlink for each chapter. (I've proposed for Podcasting 2.0 to expand chapters to support rich content, like image galleries and basic-formatted text.)

Chapters serve as a means to divide your podcast content within podcasting apps. This feature enables your audience to easily navigate through your content outline, jump to specific sections, and view the title of each chapter along with optional images and hyperlinks. (I have suggested expanding chapters in Podcasting 2.0 to support richer content, including features like image galleries and basic-formatted text.)

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You can use a different image for each chapter, and Apple Podcasts and other apps will switch to that image while that chapter plays. When a chapter is playing without an image, podcast apps will switch back to the main podcast cover art.

It is possible to utilize distinct images for each chapter, and podcast apps like Apple Podcasts will dynamically switch to display the corresponding image as the chapter is being played. In cases where a chapter does not have an assigned image, podcast apps will revert back to showcasing the primary podcast cover art.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I think it is! First, because it enhances the experience for your audience. And second, because you can use the episode artwork (along with several other layouts of the same design) to make your podcast look better when you embed it and promote it! For example, I take the exact same square artwork I put in my episode and I post it on my Instagram (@theDanielJLewis) to promote my new episode.

I believe it is beneficial for two reasons. Firstly, it improves the overall experience for your audience. Secondly, you can leverage the episode artwork, along with multiple variations of the same design, to enhance the appearance of your podcast when embedded and promoted. For instance, you can use the square artwork from your episode and share it on platforms like Instagram (@theDanielJLewis) to promote your new episode.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Seasons share many similarities with episode numbers, so please read or listen to my previous episode, “Should You Include Episode Numbers in Episode Titles?” That will give you the extra context upon which this episode will build. But we can be thankful that seasons are not as complicated as episode numbers!

To gain a better understanding of the context for this episode, I recommend reading or listening to my previous episode titled "Should You Include Episode Numbers in Episode Titles?" This will provide you with additional insight that will serve as the foundation for the current discussion. Fortunately, seasons are less complex compared to episode numbers, for which we can be grateful.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I made an episode back in 2015, “Should You Podcast in Seasons?” that I think answers the question in great detail (maybe even exhausting detail). So please go back to read or listen to that for more information. And here's a concise and updated summary of my thoughts about seasons.

In 2015, I created an episode titled "Should You Podcast in Seasons?" that offers a thorough exploration of the question at hand, perhaps even delving into exhaustive detail. I encourage you to revisit that episode to obtain a more comprehensive understanding. However, below, I provide a concise and updated summary of my thoughts on the matter.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: I recommend you use seasons only when each season follows a particular theme or arc. For example, each season of Serial told a completely different story, each season (and sometimes half season) of serial TV shows has a separate story arc, and I've heard other podcasts follow different formats in different seasons (like a “tools” season, a “top 10” season, a “client stories” season, and beginner/intermediate/advanced seasons).

My suggestion is to employ seasons solely when each season revolves around a distinct theme or storyline. Serial, for instance, featured completely different narratives in each of its seasons. Similarly, many TV series have separate story arcs for each season or even half-season. Furthermore, I've come across other podcasts that adopt varied formats for different seasons, such as dedicating a season to discussing tools, featuring a "top 10" season, sharing client stories in a specific season, or structuring seasons based on different skill levels (beginner, intermediate, advanced).

Rephrase the following text in your own words: But if you are thinking of using seasons merely for your schedule, then I don't think you should use seasons. If you need a planned hiatus, then simply announce and take the planned hiatus! There's no need to use seasons if you only need a break. Your current and future audience likely won't care about these “seasons,” and they won't even make sense to your future audience.

However, if you are considering implementing seasons solely for the purpose of managing your schedule, I would advise against it. If you require a planned break or hiatus, it is best to announce it and take the necessary time off without the need for seasons. Your current and future audience may not find the concept of "seasons" relevant or meaningful. Planning seasons based on breaks might not resonate with your listeners, particularly as it may not align with their understanding or expectations of podcast seasons.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For Apple Podcasts, the season tag is <itunes:season>, which allows only a non-zero number. For Podcasting 2.0, the tag is <podcast:season>. But, of course, the Podcasting 2.0 season tag offers more by letting you give each season a name instead of only a number. So the first season could be labeled “Getting Started,” the second season could be “Growing,” and the third season could be “Improving.”  Use both tags as well as you can until all apps upgrade to the modern Podcasting 2.0 standard.

In terms of Apple Podcasts, the season tag is denoted by <itunes:season>, which only accepts a non-zero numerical value. However, in Podcasting 2.0, the tag is <podcast:season>. Notably, the Podcasting 2.0 season tag provides added functionality by allowing the inclusion of a name for each season, rather than limiting it to just a number. This means that you can label your first season as "Getting Started," the second season as "Growing," and the third season as "Improving," for example. It is advisable to utilize both tags to the best of your ability until all podcasting apps upgrade to the modern Podcasting 2.0 standard.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: When you use these season tags, modern podcast apps can more intelligently display the season information in appropriate contexts and possibly group your episodes by season. It can even cause an entire season to download or start playing at the beginning of the latest season when you combine this with the “serial” podcast type.

By utilizing the season tags, contemporary podcast applications are able to effectively present season-related information in suitable contexts and potentially organize your episodes by season. This feature enables these apps to take actions such as downloading or initiating playback of an entire season from the beginning, particularly when combined with the "serial" podcast type. Consequently, leveraging these season tags enhances the overall functionality and user experience within modern podcasting platforms.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Podcasting 2.0 apps usually support the <podcast:season> tag and display the season name, if present. Some apps might group episodes under their seasons, like Apple Podcasts does for “serial” podcast types; and other apps might merely show the season with other meta information in the episode listing, like Pocket Casts.

Podcasting 2.0 applications typically provide support for the <podcast:season> tag and have the capability to display the corresponding season name, if specified. Certain applications may organize episodes into distinct groups based on their seasons, similar to how Apple Podcasts categorizes episodes for "serial" podcast types. On the other hand, other apps might include the season information along with other metadata in the episode listing, as observed in Pocket Casts.
